gpt-5-mini.1052832200984226985394364493169918350471:
About Precious Metals
- Buy and sell physical gold and silver via the OCBC app, available 24/7.
- You can open a Precious Metals Account instantly in the app and track your holdings.
- The app shows information for both Gold and Silver and lets you specify either an amount to invest or a quantity to buy.

Answer to your question:
- Yes — you can buy gold using OCBC’s Precious Metals service in the app. Steps (from the app guidance):
  1. Tap More > Investments > Precious Metals.
  2. View the Gold option (or Silver).
  3. If you don’t have a Precious Metals Account, open one instantly in the app.
  4. Enter your purchase details — choose between ‘Amount to invest’ or ‘Quantity’.
  5. Complete the transaction and then track your investment in the app.
- Quick question: do you already have a Precious Metals Account with OCBC, or would you like to open one now?

What to do next in the app:
- Tap the button below to open Precious Metals in the OCBC app and start buying gold.

gpt-5-mini.6354628686648720873228367623291495008501:
About Dispute Card Transactions
- A feature in OCBC Internet Banking and the OCBC app to report and dispute incorrect or unauthorised card transactions.
- Used to protect you from billing errors and potential liability for transactions you did not authorise (subject to card association rules).
- You’ll be asked to provide any supporting information or documents so OCBC can report the case to the card association (Visa or Mastercard).

Answer to your question:
- Yes — you can dispute a credit card transaction that is not yours using OCBC’s Dispute Card Transactions feature in the app.
- Report the unauthorised transaction as soon as possible: within 7 days of receiving the statement or immediately after you receive an SMS notifying you of the transaction.
- Provide any supporting information or documents when requested so OCBC can assist in reporting the unauthorised transaction to the relevant card association.
- Important points from our rules:
  - You can dispute up to 35 Visa transactions or 15 Mastercard transactions at one time.
  - There are no dispute rights for transactions that were fully authenticated (for example, authorised with an OTP or OCBC OneToken).
  - Outcomes are subject to the card association’s decision.
- If you’d like, I can help you start the dispute now in the app. Quick question: is this transaction on your OCBC card?

What to do next in the app:
- Tap the button below to open Dispute Card Transactions in the OCBC app and submit your dispute.

gpt-5-mini.8507923240348486596365445832813936977329:
Solution:
- If you are in Singapore (OCBC customer living in Singapore):
  - You can release your locked funds at any OCBC ATM, New ATM, Service Kiosk or at an OCBC branch.
  - ATM steps from the docs: Insert your ATM/debit/credit card and enter your PIN → Navigate to "More Services" → Tap "Release the funds under Money Lock" → Select the account → Tap "Confirm".

- If you live overseas (OCBC Singapore customer living overseas):
  - Submit a request to release your funds via Secured Mailbox in the OCBC app or via Internet Banking. In the Secured Mailbox, tap the "+" and select "Money Lock Release Funds", and indicate that you live overseas. OCBC will be in touch within 4 working days.

- Important conditions from the docs:
  - Locked funds must be released in full — partial releases are not allowed. If you need a smaller amount, you must release the full amount first, then submit a new lock request for the new amount.
  - You cannot release your funds at an OCBC ATM or branch while overseas.
  - Check that your card and PIN are valid before locking funds to avoid problems when releasing them.

Additional information:
- Locked funds remain in your account and continue to count towards your balance and earn interest, but they cannot be used for GIRO, scheduled/recurring payments or to pay account fees (some deductions/fees may still apply).

What you can do next:
- If in Singapore, visit any OCBC ATM/Service Kiosk or branch and follow the ATM steps above.
- If overseas, submit the Secured Mailbox request in the OCBC app or Internet Banking and await contact within 4 working days.

Solution:
- Issuance fee for a Cashier’s Order: S$5 per Cashier’s Order. (This applies whether payable to your own name or to a 3rd party.)

- Cancellation of a Cashier’s Order: S$5 per Cashier’s Order.
- Stop-payment on a Cashier’s Order: S$20 per Cashier’s Order.

Additional information:

gpt-5-mini.9565079443268160810659672963042371941926:
Solution:
- Coin deposit fee (per OCBC fees document):
  - S$2.00 for every 100 coins deposited or part thereof.
  - The same document also shows the coin deposit fee expressed as S$0.012 per coin (effective 27 April 2018); the total fee is rounded to the nearest 1 cent.  
- Coin withdrawal: S$2.00 for every S$50 withdrawn or part thereof.  
- Coin exchange: S$2.00 for every S$50 exchanged or part thereof.

Additional information:
- Coins deposited may not be credited into the account on the same day.  
- Coin deposit and exchange services are available every Tuesday and Thursday during these hours:
  - 3:00pm–5:00pm at Sunday-banking branches (e.g., Holland Village, Paya Lebar Free Square, White Sands)
  - 9:30am–11:30am at all other branches.  
- Fees are subject to prevailing GST and may change.

What you can do next:
- Bring your coins to an OCBC branch during the service hours above. Check ocbc.com for the list of Sunday-banking branches and the latest details.
gpt-5-mini.3809959158867622218299422459123248675250:
Solution:
- If you are in Singapore (recommended method: OCBC ATM / New ATM / Service Kiosk):
  1. Insert your ATM, debit or credit card and enter your PIN.
  2. Navigate to "More Services".
  3. Tap "Release the funds under Money Lock".
  4. Select the account you wish to release funds for.
  5. Tap "Confirm" to complete the release.

- If you are living overseas:
  1. Log in to the OCBC app and tap "More".
  2. Select "Secured Mailbox" and tap the "+" icon.
  3. Choose "Money Lock Release Funds" from the dropdown and state in your request that you live overseas.
  4. OCBC will respond — the docs say they will be in touch within 4 working days.

Important notes from the docs:
- Locked funds must be released in full; partial release is not allowed. If you need a smaller amount, you must release the full amount first, then lock a new amount.
- You cannot release locked funds at an OCBC ATM or branch while overseas. Singapore customers can release at local OCBC ATMs, New ATMs, Service Kiosks or at an OCBC branch.
- Locked funds remain in your account balance and continue to earn interest, but locked funds cannot be used for GIRO, scheduled/recurring payments or to pay account fees.

What you can do next:
- Do you want the ATM steps now, or are you located overseas so I should show the Secured Mailbox steps?

gpt-5-mini.3454660477932189256106449282637922866706:
About Dispute Card Transactions
- A feature in the OCBC app and Internet Banking to report and dispute incorrect, suspicious or unauthorised card transactions.
- Used to protect you from billing errors and to request investigation by the relevant card association (Visa/Mastercard).
- You’ll need to provide any supporting information or documents requested so OCBC can submit the dispute on your behalf.

Answer to your question:
- Yes — you can dispute that transaction using OCBC’s Dispute Card Transactions feature in the app or Internet Banking.
- Timeframe: report the unauthorised transaction within 7 days of receiving the statement, or immediately when you get an SMS alert for the transaction.
- What OCBC needs / rules from the docs:
  - Provide the information or supporting documents requested so OCBC can assist in reporting to the card association.
  - Fraud-dispute limits: you can dispute up to 35 Visa transactions or 15 Mastercard transactions in one go.
  - There are no dispute rights for transactions that were fully authenticated (for example, via OTP or OCBC OneToken). Final decisions rest with the card association.
- If this is a suspected fraud, use the in-app dispute feature so OCBC can investigate — do not share PINs, passwords or OTPs.

Clarifying question:
- Did you receive an SMS alert or the card statement showing this transaction? (This helps determine urgency and the reporting timeframe.)

What to do next in the app:
- Tap the button below to open Dispute Card Transactions in the OCBC app and start your dispute.

gpt-5-mini.2319782525846211422507447987034733690520:
About Dispute Card Transactions
- A feature in OCBC Internet Banking and the OCBC app that lets you report and dispute incorrect, suspicious or unauthorised card transactions.
- Use it to ask OCBC to investigate billing errors and to help report suspected fraud to the relevant card associations (Visa or Mastercard).
- Important limits & conditions from OCBC: report within 7 days of receiving the statement or immediately after getting an SMS about the transaction; you can dispute up to 35 Visa or 15 Mastercard transactions at one time; there are no dispute rights for transactions fully authenticated with OTP or OCBC OneToken, or for transactions made in person with a lost/stolen card. Final outcomes are subject to the card association’s decision.
- When you dispute, OCBC may ask you to provide supporting information/documents — please keep these ready.

Answer to your question:
- If you see a card transaction that is not yours, it could be a merchant/billing error or an unauthorised/fraudulent charge. You should report it as soon as possible so OCBC can investigate and, if appropriate, raise it with Visa/Mastercard on your behalf.
- Report the transaction via OCBC Internet Banking or the OCBC app (the dispute feature is available in both). Do so within 7 days of receiving the statement that shows the charge, or immediately if you received an SMS alert about the transaction.
- Note: if the transaction was fully authenticated (for example via OTP or OCBC OneToken) or was made in person with a lost/stolen card, dispute rights may not apply.
- OCBC will request supporting documents where needed — please provide them so they can assist you.
- Quick question to help you proceed: did you see the transaction on your card statement or receive an SMS alert about it? (If yes, when did you receive it?)

What to do next in the app:
- Tap the button below to open Dispute Card Transactions in the OCBC app — you can start your dispute there and follow the prompts to submit the transaction details and any supporting documents.
gpt-5-mini.1130525731156713318373573723006218633191:
Solution:
- If you are in Singapore (OCBC customer living in Singapore)
  1. Go to any OCBC ATM, New ATM or Service Kiosk (or visit an OCBC branch).
  2. Insert your ATM/debit/credit card and enter your PIN.
  3. Navigate to "More Services" → tap "Release the funds under Money Lock".
  4. Select the account to release and tap "Confirm".

- If you are an OCBC Singapore customer living overseas
  1. Log in to the OCBC app (or Internet Banking).
  2. Go to "More" → "Secured Mailbox" → tap the "+" icon.
  3. Choose "Money Lock Release Funds" from the dropdown and state that you live overseas.
  4. OCBC will contact you; the docs say they will be in touch within 4 working days.

Additional information
- You cannot release your funds at an OCBC ATM or branch when you are overseas.
- Locked funds must be released in full; partial release is not allowed. After releasing in full, you may re-lock a new amount if needed.
- If you plan to travel and may need access to locked funds, the docs encourage releasing them at an OCBC ATM before you leave.
- You may locate the nearest OCBC ATM/New ATM/Service Kiosk using the ATM locator provided in the app/website.

What you can do next
- If you’re in Singapore: visit an OCBC ATM, New ATM, Service Kiosk or branch and follow the ATM steps above.
- If you’re overseas: submit the "Money Lock Release Funds" request via Secured Mailbox in the OCBC app or Internet Banking and wait for the bank to contact you (per the docs, within 4 working days).
